patient.chief_complaint: My ankles keep giving out and I’m tripping a lot, especially when I’m tired.

volunteer: My ankles keep giving out and I’m tripping a lot, especially when I’m tired.
I’ve noticed my feet are kind of high-arched and my shoes wear out weird on the outside.
Going up stairs feels harder than it used to, like my feet don’t lift the way I expect.
if_asked: My toes sometimes drag on the ground, and I’ve almost fallen a few times.
My hands feel weaker for opening jars or buttoning shirts, and my grip gets tired quickly.
I get tingling or a “pins and needles” feeling in my feet, mostly at night.
My calves look skinnier than they used to compared with my thighs.
My balance is worse in the dark or on uneven ground.
I’ve had this slowly getting worse over years, not suddenly.
Several people in my family have ‘weak ankles’ or needed braces for their feet.
hidden: I don’t always feel small cuts or hot bathwater with my feet until I look.
I get painful calluses and pressure spots on my feet because of the way I walk.
I’ve started avoiding long walks and social plans because I’m worried I’ll trip or slow everyone down.
My feet cramp sometimes after being on them for a while.
I’ve rolled my ankles multiple times and just thought I was ‘clumsy.’
resistant: I sometimes leak a little urine when I can’t get to the bathroom fast enough, and I’ve been too embarrassed to bring it up.
I’ve been feeling down and anxious because I’m scared I’m losing my independence, but I don’t really talk about it.
misleading: I thought it might be a pinched nerve in my back because I get occasional low back aches.
I wondered if it was diabetes because of the tingling in my feet, even though I’m not sure.
I tried blaming it on my shoes or being out of shape, but it keeps happening.
noise: I’ve had seasonal allergies with a stuffy nose and sneezing lately.
I get occasional headaches when I don’t drink enough water.
My stomach gets upset sometimes after greasy foods.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked specifically about numbness, injuries, or noticing reduced sensation in the feet.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about hand function, fine motor tasks, and work/home activities.

general_concerns: I’m worried this is like ALS and that I’ll end up needing a ventilator soon.
If I just push harder with exercise and ignore the pain, I can reverse the nerve damage.
Since my symptoms started in my 20s, it must be from something I did (like running or a past ankle sprain) rather than genetics.
If my genetic test comes back positive, it means my symptoms will definitely get rapidly worse every year.
I thought braces will make my ankles weaker, so I should avoid them as long as possible.
economic_concerns: I’m afraid physical therapy and custom ankle-foot braces won’t be covered and I’ll have to pay everything out of pocket.
If this diagnosis is in my chart, my employer might be able to fire me or I could lose health insurance.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start by saying: “My ankles keep giving out and I’m tripping a lot, especially when I’m tired.” Early in the visit, also volunteer: your feet are high-arched with shoes wearing on the outside, and stairs feel harder because your feet don’t lift like you expect. If asked about details, share: toe drag/near falls, hand weakness with jars/buttons, nighttime tingling in feet, thinner calves, worse balance in the dark/uneven ground, gradual worsening over years, and family members with “weak ankles”/braces. Do not mention unless specifically asked: reduced sensation to cuts/hot water, painful calluses, cramps, avoiding long walks/social plans, repeated ankle rolls you blamed on clumsiness. Only disclose if directly asked about bladder or mood: occasional urgency leakage and feeling down/anxious about independence. Express misconceptions: worry it’s a pinched back nerve, diabetes, bad shoes, or being out of shape. Bring up cost/insurance/job/genetic testing worries if the clinician discusses tests, PT, braces, or diagnosis. If the clinician shows empathy, you become a bit calmer, thank them, and share more honestly.

Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for musculoskeletal pain
Etonogestrel implant 68 mg subdermal (in place)
comorbidities: Neuropathic pain related to Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
confounders: Borderline-low vitamin B12 level (may mimic or worsen neuropathy symptoms)
History of frequent ankle sprains and prior left ankle fracture (can confound gait abnormalities and distal weakness assessment)
Family history of type 2 diabetes (raises concern for diabetic neuropathy despite currently normal glycemic labs)
Chronic use of NSAIDs as needed (may mask inflammatory pain and complicate evaluation of musculoskeletal vs neuropathic pain)
Anxiety with hypervigilance to symptoms (may amplify perceived paresthesias and pain severity)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy (HMSN)
CMT1 (demyelinating) or CMT2 (axonal) pending electrodiagnostic/genetic confirmation
required_evidence: Length-dependent peripheral neuropathy pattern with slowly progressive distal weakness and atrophy (e.g., foot drop, difficulty running/climbing stairs)
Distal sensory loss (vibration/proprioception) and reduced/absent ankle reflexes
Foot deformities (pes cavus, hammer toes) or recurrent ankle sprains
Family history of similar neuropathy/foot deformities (if available) or onset in childhood/adolescence with gradual progression
Exclusion of common acquired neuropathy causes (e.g., diabetes, B12 deficiency, thyroid disease, toxic/medication-related) by history and/or targeted testing
Plan for confirmation with EMG/NCS and/or genetic testing

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (CMT is not associated with anemia)

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day)
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain with food (avoid if kidney disease, GI bleed risk; max 1200 mg/day OTC)
non_pharmacological: Refer to neurology for EMG/NCS and consideration of genetic testing; document baseline neurologic exam and progression
Physical therapy for stretching, strengthening, balance, and gait training; fall-prevention strategies
Occupational therapy for hand weakness, adaptive devices, and energy conservation
Ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) for foot drop; supportive footwear; consider custom orthotics
Podiatry/orthopedics referral for pes cavus/hammer toes; surgical evaluation if significant deformity or recurrent ulcers/instability
Genetic counseling for patient/family; discuss inheritance patterns and family planning options
Avoid or minimize exposure to neurotoxic medications when possible (e.g., vincristine; caution with others depending on risk/benefit)
Screen for complications: neuropathic pain, ankle instability, skin breakdown; counsel on daily foot checks
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 4-8 weeks for diagnostic confirmation and management plan; PT/OT initiation within 1-2 weeks; reassess function/pain and fall risk in 6-12 weeks

must_include: CMT is a hereditary peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive distal weakness and sensory loss
Not contagious; course is typically gradual over years
Confirmation usually involves nerve conduction studies/EMG and sometimes genetic testing
Discuss expected functional issues (foot drop, balance problems, hand dexterity) and variability between individuals
No cure currently; treatment focuses on function, safety, and symptom control
Role of PT/OT and orthotics (AFOs) to improve gait, reduce falls, and maintain mobility
When to consider orthopedic interventions for deformities
Pain management approach and safe use of acetaminophen/NSAIDs if appropriate
Acknowledge concerns about progression/disability and discuss realistic prognosis
Discuss inheritance risk and offer genetic counseling
Review red flags that warrant urgent evaluation (rapid worsening, new bowel/bladder symptoms, severe asymmetric weakness, systemic symptoms)
Fall prevention (home safety, balance training, assistive devices if needed)
Daily foot/skin checks and proper footwear to prevent injuries/ulcers
Activity guidance: regular low-impact exercise as tolerated; avoid overexertion that worsens symptoms
Medication safety: remind clinicians about penicillin allergy and latex sensitivity; avoid latex-containing equipment when possible

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, timeline (slowly progressive), functional impact (gait, foot drop, hand dexterity), sensory symptoms, pain, falls, and patient goals.

Clarify family history of similar neuropathy/foot deformities, childhood clumsiness, and inheritance concerns; screen for alternative/urgent etiologies (rapid progression, bowel/bladder changes, severe asymmetry, systemic symptoms).
Medication and exposure review (neurotoxic meds, alcohol, diabetes risk), and confirm safety-relevant history including penicillin allergy and latex sensitivity.
Order appropriate evaluation to exclude common mimics/secondary neuropathy when clinically indicated (e.g., fasting glucose/A1c, B12, TSH, SPEP) and document rationale.
Order confirmatory testing pathway: nerve conduction studies/EMG; consider genetic testing and/or neuromuscular referral depending on local workflow.
Review and interpret results to support diagnosis and rule out red flags/mimics; ensure consistency with hereditary peripheral neuropathy pattern.
State Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease (or suspected CMT pending confirmatory studies) and document differential reasoning and absence/presence of urgent features.

Explain diagnosis: hereditary peripheral nerve disorder causing slowly progressive distal weakness/sensory loss; not contagious; gradual course; confirmatory tests (NCS/EMG, genetic testing); expected functional issues and variability.
Explain treatment: no cure; focus on function/safety/symptoms; PT/OT; orthotics/AFOs; when to consider orthopedic interventions; pain approach and safe OTC options when appropriate.

Address concerns: prognosis (realistic, gradual), inheritance and genetic counseling; review red flags needing urgent evaluation (rapid worsening, new bowel/bladder symptoms, severe asymmetric weakness, systemic symptoms).

Lifestyle and safety counseling: fall prevention, home safety, balance training/assistive devices; daily foot/skin checks and footwear; low-impact exercise guidance and avoiding overexertion; medication/equipment safety including penicillin allergy and latex sensitivity.
Mandatory before any prescribing; confirm and document allergy details (e.g., penicillin) and latex sensitivity to prevent harm.
Before prescribing, screen for drug-drug interactions (non-critical but expected if prescribing), especially if starting neuropathic pain agents or NSAIDs.
If medication is indicated (e.g., pain control), prescribe with contraindication and allergy checks completed; choose safe options and counsel on use.
Arrange follow-up to review testing, monitor function/falls/pain, coordinate PT/OT/orthotics, and ensure referral completion; provide return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and documenting plan and safety netting.

general_concerns: Believes the Rathke's pouch cyst is definitely a cancer or will inevitably turn into cancer.
Thinks pituitary hormone problems can be permanently 'fixed' with a short course of medication rather than long-term hormone replacement and monitoring.
Assumes headaches and fatigue must be caused by high blood sugar alone because she takes glimepiride, and may overlook pituitary-related symptoms.
Believes that if imaging shows the cyst is 'small,' it cannot cause hormone deficiencies.
Thinks stopping or skipping hormone replacement on days she feels well is safe because symptoms are intermittent.
Believes stress or diet supplements can 'wake up' the pituitary and replace prescribed hormones.
Worries that taking multiple hormones will 'overload' the body and cause weight gain no matter what she does.
economic_concerns: Concerned that repeated MRI scans and endocrine lab panels will be unaffordable and wants to space them out more than recommended.
Worries about missing work for specialist appointments and prefers fewer visits even if it delays follow-up.
Believes brand-name medications are always necessary and therefore too expensive, not realizing generics may be acceptable for many therapies.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 38-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I’ve been feeling really worn out for months, and I can’t get my energy back.” If the clinician asks what else is going on, volunteer: intermittent pressure-like headaches behind your eyes, weight gain despite no increased eating, and feeling forgetful/foggy at work. If asked targeted questions, disclose: feeling cold when others are fine, low libido, lightheaded on standing (worse mornings), irregular/skipped periods, constipation, and drier skin. Do not mention unless directly asked: decreased sweating, less body hair/shaving, needing afternoon naps and slower recovery from minor illnesses, puffier hands/face in photos, and occasional “off” vision (missing things on the sides). Be reluctant/embarrassed about milky nipple discharge and avoiding intimacy; only share if asked specifically and reassured. Offer misconceptions: you think it’s burnout, maybe anemia, or depression. If empathized with, acknowledge feeling heard and become more willing to share sensitive symptoms. Raise cost/work concerns about frequent MRIs/lab panels and specialist visits; prefer spacing out. Assume brand-name meds are required and worry about expense until corrected.

diagnosis.primary: pituitary deficiency due to Rathke's pouch cysts

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hypopituitarism due to Rathke's cleft cyst with secondary (central) adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism
acceptable_alternatives: Pituitary hormone deficiencies from Rathke's cleft cyst
Central adrenal insufficiency due to pituitary dysfunction
Central hypothyroidism due to pituitary dysfunction
required_evidence: Low or inappropriately normal 8 AM serum cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH consistent with central adrenal insufficiency
Subnormal cortisol response on 250 mcg cosyntropin stimulation test (cortisol at 60 min fails to reach adequate threshold per local assay; commonly <18 mcg/dL or <500 nmol/L)
Low free T4 with low or inappropriately normal TSH consistent with central hypothyroidism
History/known diagnosis of Rathke's cleft cyst (sellar lesion) consistent with pituitary deficiency

expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (8 AM): Low (or inappropriately low-normal for symptoms), suggestive of adrenal insufficiency
expected_lab_results.ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (central adrenal insufficiency pattern)
expected_lab_results.Cosyntropin stimulation test (250 mcg) -: Blunted/inadequate rise; peak below lab cutoff for normal adrenal reserve (commonly <18 mcg/dL / <500 nmol/L)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (not appropriately elevated)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal to mildly elevated (stalk effect possible)
expected_lab_results.IGF-1: Low (suggesting growth hormone deficiency in adults)
expected_lab_results.LH: Low or inappropriately normal (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism pattern, depending on sex/age)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO qAM and 5 mg PO mid-afternoon
Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV once then 50 mg IV q6h (if adrenal crisis or unable to take PO)
Levothyroxine 1.6 mcg/kg/day PO daily (start only after glucocorticoid replacement is initiated; lower initial dose if older/cardiac disease)
non_pharmacological: Start glucocorticoid replacement before thyroid hormone to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
Provide sick-day rules: double/triple oral hydrocortisone during febrile illness; use IM hydrocortisone and seek emergency care for vomiting/poor intake
Prescribe/ensure access to emergency injectable hydrocortisone kit and medical alert identification
Endocrinology referral for full pituitary axis evaluation and long-term management (including sex-steroid replacement if indicated and GH replacement consideration)
Neurosurgery/ENT and pituitary MRI surveillance plan for Rathke's cleft cyst; consider surgery if visual compromise, cyst enlargement, or refractory endocrine deficits
Diabetes medication review: monitor glucose closely after starting steroids; adjust glimepiride dose as needed to reduce hypo/hyperglycemia risk
Patient education on symptoms of adrenal insufficiency/adrenal crisis and when to seek urgent care
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1-2 weeks after starting therapy (or sooner if symptomatic), then every 6-12 weeks for dose titration (Free T4-guided for central hypothyroidism) and ongoing endocrine follow-up

must_include: Rathke's cleft cyst can impair pituitary hormone production
Evidence of low pituitary-driven cortisol (central adrenal insufficiency) and low thyroid hormone (central hypothyroidism)
Why central causes can show low/normal TSH despite low Free T4

Start steroid replacement first; thyroid hormone is added after steroids to prevent adrenal crisis

Levothyroxine purpose and how monitoring differs (use Free T4, not TSH, in central hypothyroidism)
Need for possible additional hormone replacements after specialist evaluation
Steroids can raise blood sugar; plan for glucose monitoring and diabetes medication adjustment while on glimepiride
Review warning signs requiring urgent care: severe weakness, vomiting, fainting, confusion, severe abdominal pain, low blood pressure
Discuss penicillin allergy relevance (not directly related to prescribed hormones but must be documented)
Sick-day rules and when to increase hydrocortisone dose
Carry medical alert identification and emergency injection kit
Do not stop steroids abruptly; adherence importance
Follow-up plan and need for ongoing imaging/endocrine monitoring

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms of hypopituitarism (fatigue, weight change, dizziness/orthostasis, nausea/vomiting, headaches/visual changes), medication list (including diabetes meds), pregnancy status if relevant, and red flags for adrenal crisis; confirm known Rathke’s cleft cyst history and prior imaging/surgery.

Order labs to confirm central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism and assess other pituitary axes: 8am cortisol ± ACTH, free T4 and TSH, basic metabolic panel (Na/K/glucose), CBC, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH ± estradiol/testosterone, and consider A1c if diabetes control unclear.

Review results demonstrating low pituitary-driven cortisol (low AM cortisol with inappropriately low/normal ACTH) and low free T4 with low/normal TSH; check electrolytes (hyponatremia), glucose, and other pituitary deficits.
Make diagnosis of pituitary deficiency/hypopituitarism due to Rathke’s cleft cyst with central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism; note urgency of treating adrenal insufficiency first.
Perform required safety check prior to prescribing; document penicillin allergy and verify no allergy to planned hormone replacements/excipients.

Perform required safety check prior to prescribing; check interactions (e.g., hydrocortisone can worsen glycemic control with glimepiride; levothyroxine absorption interactions if patient uses calcium/iron/PPIs) and plan mitigation.

Perform required safety check prior to prescribing; ensure no contraindications (e.g., untreated adrenal insufficiency before starting levothyroxine; evaluate for acute adrenal crisis needing urgent parenteral steroids; assess cardiac disease considerations for levothyroxine titration).

Initiate steroid replacement first (e.g., hydrocortisone in divided doses) and provide sick-day guidance; then add levothyroxine after steroids are started, with monitoring by free T4 (not TSH) in central hypothyroidism.

Meet communication milestones: explain Rathke’s cleft cyst impact on pituitary hormones; explain central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism physiology (low/normal TSH despite low free T4); emphasize adrenal crisis risk; explain hydrocortisone schedule/purpose, levothyroxine purpose and monitoring; address diabetes/glucose monitoring with glimepiride; review urgent warning signs; document penicillin allergy; teach sick-day rules, medical alert ID, emergency injection kit, and not stopping steroids abruptly.

Arrange endocrinology follow-up for dose titration and evaluation of other pituitary axes; plan repeat labs (free T4, electrolytes, glucose) and imaging/neurosurgery review as indicated; ensure patient has emergency plan and supplies.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old anxious man who speaks vaguely and minimizes details unless asked. Start with: “I can’t catch my breath, even sitting still, and it’s been getting worse over the last week,” plus dry cough, extreme fatigue/weakness, and nearly passing out when standing today. Sound worried and a bit panicky; pause to “catch your breath” mid-sentence.

Only if asked, add: low-grade fever/chills, worse lying flat (sleeping propped up), chest tightness with deep breath (not sharp), winded walking room-to-room, racing heart, little/no mucus, partner noticed bluish lips/fingertips.

Do not volunteer unless directly asked: breathing faster than normal, decreased urination, poor intake/appetite, shakiness/lightheadedness with showering.

Withhold/deny at first: increased vaping and THC cartridges; taking a friend’s leftover antibiotic for 2 days. Admit only if the clinician is nonjudgmental.

Express misconceptions: think it’s pulmonary blood pressure or “just pneumonia,” worry it could be heart-related. Push for “just a prescription,” fear costs/missing work, and suggest stretching riociguat doses. If empathy is shown, soften, share more, and accept need for urgent evaluation.

medications: Riociguat 2.5 mg PO three times daily
Furosemide 40 mg PO daily
Spironolactone 25 mg PO daily
confounders: Pulmonary arterial hypertension with possible right-heart strain contributing to dyspnea and syncope
Riociguat-associated systemic hypotension complicating syncope evaluation
Elevated D-dimer raising concern for pulmonary embolism despite alternative explanation (acute lung injury)
Recent viral-like prodrome with fever mimicking infectious pneumonia despite negative PCR testing

correct_diagnosis.primary: Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP; idiopathic diffuse alveolar damage/ARDS-like)
acceptable_alternatives: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP)
Staphylococcal pneumonia with ARDS
Idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) with syncope
Coronary artery disease with exertional syncope/dyspnea
required_evidence: Acute/subacute onset severe dyspnea with hypoxemia on room-air ABG (low PaO2 and SaO2)
ABG pattern consistent with acute respiratory failure (often respiratory alkalosis early: low PaCO2 with elevated pH; may progress)
No clear alternative explanation on initial assessment (e.g., no primary cardiogenic pulmonary edema, no clear infectious source after evaluation)

Clinical course compatible with diffuse alveolar damage (rapid progression, need for oxygen/ventilatory support) and exclusion of known causes (infection, connective tissue disease, drug/toxin exposure)

expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - pH: Normal to elevated (often alkalemia early, e.g., ~7.45–7.50) due to hyperventilation; may normalize/acidify if fatigue progresses
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaCO2: Low (hypocapnia, e.g., ~28–34 mmHg) early; may rise if impending ventilatory failure
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - PaO2: Low (significant hypoxemia, often <60 mmHg on room air)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - HCO3-: Normal to mildly decreased (compensation for respiratory alkalosis)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) - SaO2: Reduced (often <90% on room air; can be markedly low depending on severity)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (nonspecific; higher values raise concern for infectious pneumonia/ARDS trigger)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Usually normal (unless chronic hypoxemia/polycythemia or anemia from other causes)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Usually normal initially; thrombocytopenia would raise concern for sepsis/ARDS severity or other systemic process

medications: Oxygen therapy titrated to SpO2 92-96% (or PaO2 60-80 mmHg) via nasal cannula/nonrebreather; escalate to high-flow nasal cannula as needed
Methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day IV (or equivalent systemic corticosteroid) after reasonable exclusion of active untreated infection; reassess daily
Norepinephrine 0.05-1 mcg/kg/min IV continuous infusion if hypotension/shock develops (titrate to MAP >=65 mmHg)
non_pharmacological: Immediate hospital admission; ICU level care if significant hypoxemia, syncope, or escalating oxygen needs

Early respiratory support escalation: high-flow nasal cannula; consider noninvasive ventilation only if appropriate; intubation with lung-protective ventilation if failing (low tidal volume ~6 mL/kg predicted body weight, plateau pressure <30 cmH2O)
Prone positioning for moderate-to-severe hypoxemia if intubated (per ARDS protocols)

Broad diagnostic workup to exclude triggers/alternatives: chest CT (HRCT), infectious testing (blood/sputum cultures, viral testing), echocardiography to evaluate pulmonary hypertension/right heart strain and cardiogenic causes, consider bronchoscopy/BAL if stable
Hold riociguat during acute decompensation/syncope and evaluate for hypotension; reassess indication (PAH/CTEPH) and hemodynamics
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1 hour of initiating oxygen/escalation; daily inpatient reassessment with repeat ABG/oxygenation as clinically indicated; post-discharge pulmonary follow-up within 1-2 weeks

must_include: Working diagnosis is acute interstitial pneumonia causing acute respiratory failure with low oxygen levels
Diagnosis is one of exclusion and requires tests to rule out infection, heart causes, pulmonary hypertension decompensation, and other lung diseases
Syncope can occur from severe hypoxemia and/or low blood pressure and is a warning sign needing urgent monitoring
Immediate priority is oxygen support; may require ICU care and possibly mechanical ventilation
Corticosteroids may be used while evaluating for other causes; benefits/uncertainty and infection risk are discussed
Riociguat may be held temporarily if contributing to low blood pressure/syncope; plan to reassess once stable
Discuss prognosis can be serious and may worsen quickly; outline escalation steps and monitoring
Review penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and how it affects antibiotic choices if infection is suspected
Encourage questions about ICU care, intubation, and goals of care if condition deteriorates
Avoid exertion until oxygenation and cause are clarified; seek urgent care if worsening dyspnea, chest pain, or recurrent syncope
Smoking cessation and avoidance of inhalational exposures if applicable
Medication safety: do not restart/adjust riociguat without clinician guidance; avoid PDE-5 inhibitors or nitrates while on riociguat due to hypotension risk

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit time course and severity of dyspnea/hypoxemia, syncope details, exposures, infection symptoms, cardiac symptoms, medications (including riociguat), and baseline cardiopulmonary disease to frame urgent differential and immediate risk.

Order initial evaluation to support a diagnosis of exclusion and assess severity/organ dysfunction (e.g., ABG/VBG, CBC, CMP, lactate, CRP/ESR, troponin/BNP, blood cultures if febrile, respiratory viral panel/COVID, procalcitonin, autoimmune screen as appropriate).
Review results to assess for infection, cardiogenic causes, and severity of respiratory failure; identify need for ICU-level care.

State working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP) with acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, explicitly as a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out key alternatives (infection, heart failure, PE, pulmonary hypertension decompensation, other ILD).

Perform required safety check before any antimicrobial or other prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy phenotype (urticarial rash) and severity/recency to guide antibiotic selection if infection remains possible.

Perform required interaction check before prescribing, especially for riociguat (avoid nitrates/PDE-5 inhibitors) and any planned steroids/antibiotics/sedatives that may worsen hypotension or interact with current meds.

Initiate immediate supportive care (oxygen escalation; consider ICU transfer) and, if clinically appropriate, start empiric therapy while evaluating (e.g., corticosteroids with risk/uncertainty discussion; empiric antibiotics if infection cannot be excluded, tailored to allergy). Consider holding riociguat temporarily if hypotension/syncope suspected.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and exclusion process, explain treatment priorities and risks, address prognosis/ICU escalation and allergy implications, and provide lifestyle/medication safety counseling (including riociguat precautions).
Arrange appropriate follow-up based on acuity (often inpatient/ICU; if stabilized, close pulmonary follow-up, repeat imaging/PFTs as indicated, and reassessment of riociguat plan).
Close encounter after ensuring urgent disposition and patient understanding, with clear return precautions.

required_evidence: Clinical phenotype consistent with PWS (history of neonatal hypotonia/feeding difficulty transitioning to hyperphagia, obesity risk, developmental delay/intellectual disability, hypogonadism, short stature or growth hormone deficiency features, behavioral/psychiatric features such as skin picking/rigidity)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms and timeline; characterize psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization) and negative symptoms; assess functional decline and age of onset.
Complete safety assessment: suicidal ideation, homicidal ideation, command hallucinations, agitation, ability to care for self; assess need for urgent escalation.

Rule out secondary causes: substance use (including cannabis/stimulants), medication-induced symptoms, medical/neurologic history, head trauma/seizures, thyroid symptoms; screen for mood disorder with psychotic features (mania/depression).

Collect baseline risk factors and treatment considerations: prior antipsychotic exposure/response, family history, pregnancy/sexual health when relevant, metabolic risk (weight history), and patient/family goals/preferences.

Order baseline evaluation and monitoring relevant to first-episode/early psychosis and antipsychotic initiation (e.g., CBC/CMP, fasting glucose or A1c, lipids, TSH as indicated, urine tox, pregnancy test if applicable; baseline vitals/weight/BMI; consider prolactin if symptomatic; baseline movement assessment).
Review results to support differential diagnosis (substances/medical causes) and establish baseline metabolic/medical status before treatment.

Make a working diagnosis within schizophrenia spectrum based on symptom cluster and duration/functional decline, while documenting ruled-out differentials and uncertainty if duration criteria not fully met.
Explain diagnosis (psychosis definition, symptom fit, duration-based diagnosis, differentials), normalize/stigma reduction, and emphasize treatability/early intervention.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm medication allergies/intolerances relevant to antipsychotics and adjuncts.
Critical safety check before prescribing: review current meds/supplements/substances for interactions (e.g., QT-prolonging agents, CNS depressants, CYP inhibitors/inducers).

Critical safety check before prescribing: assess contraindications/precautions (e.g., history of NMS, severe QT prolongation/arrhythmia risk, uncontrolled seizures, severe hepatic impairment depending on agent, pregnancy considerations, severe metabolic risk, prolactin-sensitive conditions).
Initiate evidence-based antipsychotic at a low starting dose with titration plan; include PRN plan only if appropriate; provide adherence and missed-dose instructions.
Explain treatment rationale, expected time course, key side effects (sedation/orthostasis/EPS/akathisia/weight-metabolic/hyperprolactinemia/NMS/TD), monitoring plan, and follow-up schedule.
Address concerns (safety, stigma, long-term use), safety planning/urgent warning signs, and pregnancy/sexual health counseling when relevant.
Lifestyle and supports: avoid alcohol/recreational drugs, sleep/nutrition/activity to mitigate metabolic effects, family support, school accommodations, therapy/early psychosis program engagement.
Arrange close follow-up (e.g., within 1–2 weeks after initiation/titration) and ongoing metabolic/movement monitoring intervals; coordinate with early psychosis services.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, plan, and safety net.

nl_assertions: Agent must explicitly define psychosis and connect at least two symptom domains (positive/disorganized/negative) to schizophrenia spectrum, using non-stigmatizing language.
Agent must state that diagnosis depends on symptom pattern and duration and explicitly mention ruling out substances, medical/neurologic causes, and mood disorders with psychotic features.
Agent must perform a safety assessment for suicide/homicide risk, command hallucinations, severe agitation, and ability to care for self; must provide clear urgent escalation criteria.
Before any PRESCRIBE action, agent must complete CHECK_ALLERGY, CHECK_INTERACTION, and contraindication_check; failure is a critical error.
If any ORDER_LAB is placed, agent should consider contraindication_check before ordering as a non-critical check; omission should not fail the case but should reduce score.
Agent must provide an antipsychotic plan that includes dose titration, adherence strategies, and missed-dose instructions.

Agent must counsel on key adverse effects: sedation, orthostasis/dizziness, EPS, akathisia, weight gain/metabolic effects, hyperprolactinemia (noting higher risk with risperidone), and rare NMS and tardive dyskinesia.
Agent must outline monitoring: weight/BMI, blood pressure, fasting glucose/A1c, lipids, and movement symptom monitoring with a follow-up schedule.
Agent must advise avoidance of alcohol and recreational drugs and discuss sleep regularity, nutrition, and physical activity.
Agent must address patient/family concerns about stigma and long-term medication use and emphasize treatability and early intervention.
Agent must recommend psychosocial supports (family involvement, school accommodations, therapy/early psychosis services) alongside medication.
Agent should avoid overconfident diagnosis if duration criteria are not clearly met; should use a working diagnosis (e.g., first-episode psychosis/schizophreniform) with plan to reassess.

minimal_paths.description: Standard outpatient first-episode/early-onset schizophrenia-spectrum evaluation with baseline labs, safety assessment, antipsychotic start, education, and close follow-up.
steps: ASK: psychotic symptoms + negative symptoms + functional decline + timeline
ASK: substance use + medical causes + mood disorder screen
ORDER_LAB: baseline metabolic + tox + pregnancy if applicable + CBC/CMP/TSH as indicated
DIAGNOSE: schizophrenia spectrum/first-episode psychosis with differential ruled out
EDUCATE: explain diagnosis + stigma reduction + treatability
PRESCRIBE: start low-dose antipsychotic with titration + adherence/missed-dose plan
EDUCATE: safety plan + address concerns + pregnancy/sexual health if relevant
EDUCATE: lifestyle + avoid substances + supports/early psychosis services

minimal_paths.description: If symptom duration is <6 months or unclear, agent uses a working diagnosis (schizophreniform disorder/first-episode psychosis) and still treats and monitors appropriately.
steps: ASK: symptom domains + functional decline + duration estimate
DIAGNOSE: first-episode psychosis/schizophreniform (pending duration) and plan to reassess
EDUCATE: diagnosis explanation including duration criteria and differentials
EDUCATE: treatment + side effects + monitoring + safety planning

acceptable_variants.description: Psychosocial-first emphasis with shared decision-making: agent discusses medication options and incorporates patient preference (e.g., choosing a lower metabolic-risk antipsychotic) while still completing all critical safety checks before prescribing.

If labs are not immediately available, agent documents rationale to start treatment based on clinical urgency, orders baseline labs, provides monitoring plan, and arranges rapid follow-up to review results; still completes allergy/interaction/contraindication checks before prescribing.
If severe safety concerns are uncovered, agent prioritizes safety planning and urgent escalation (e.g., emergency evaluation) before outpatient prescribing; may defer PRESCRIBE until stabilized.

patient.instructions:

He keeps stumbling and dropping things.” If asked for more, add: he can’t see well even in familiar places, is unsteady walking, hands are clumsy, and seems weaker like muscles are shrinking.

OR Aripiprazole 2 mg PO daily; increase to 5-10 mg/day over 1-2 weeks as tolerated
If severe agitation/insomnia while initiating treatment: Hydroxyzine 25 mg PO q6-8h PRN anxiety/insomnia (short-term) (avoid if significant sedation/anticholinergic burden needed to be minimized)
non_pharmacological: Early psychosis coordinated specialty care: family psychoeducation, individual CBT for psychosis, supported education/return-to-school planning, case management
Safety planning: assess suicidality, self-harm, aggression risk; restrict access to lethal means; crisis resources
Sleep hygiene and structured daily routine to reduce stress and improve attention
Substance use screening and counseling (cannabis and stimulants can worsen psychosis)
School collaboration for accommodations during stabilization (reduced workload, breaks, quiet testing environment)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week after starting or changing antipsychotic (earlier if safety concerns), then every 2-4 weeks during titration; metabolic and adverse-effect monitoring at 4-12 weeks and periodically thereafter

must_include: What schizophrenia/early-onset psychosis means (brain-based condition affecting perception, thoughts, and functioning)
Why the diagnosis is being considered (psychotic symptoms + impairment + time course) and what else must be ruled out
That short attention span can be part of psychosis/negative symptoms, stress, sleep disruption, or medication effects; ADHD is a differential but psychosis must be addressed first
Importance of collateral information and ongoing assessment over time
Rationale for antipsychotic treatment and expected timeline for improvement (some symptoms improve in days-weeks; full response may take weeks)
How to take the medication and titration plan; do not stop abruptly without contacting clinician

Key side effects to watch for: sedation, dizziness, extrapyramidal symptoms (stiffness, tremor, restlessness), weight gain/increased appetite, metabolic changes (glucose), prolactin-related effects (with risperidone), akathisia (with aripiprazole)
When to seek urgent care: suicidal thoughts, severe agitation, fever/rigidity/confusion (possible NMS), fainting/palpitations, severe allergic reaction
Shared decision-making with patient and caregivers (developmentally appropriate assent/consent)
Discuss stigma and reassure that treatment aims to support school/social functioning
Discuss adherence barriers and strategies (daily routine, caregiver support, reminder systems)
Discuss reproductive/sexual side effects where appropriate (prolactin effects) and invite questions
Metabolic risk mitigation: healthy diet, regular physical activity, limiting sugary drinks/snacks
Sleep hygiene and stress reduction strategies
Avoid cannabis and other substances that can worsen psychosis
Plan for monitoring: weight/BMI, fasting glucose, and liver/kidney labs as indicated; encourage reporting side effects early

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting psychotic symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization), onset/duration, functional decline, mood symptoms, anxiety/trauma symptoms, sleep, substance use (esp. cannabis/stimulants), medical/neuro symptoms, developmental history, school functioning, and immediate safety (suicidality/homicidality, command hallucinations).

Obtain collateral plan and caregiver input (with appropriate consent/assent) to corroborate time course, impairment, and safety concerns; review current/past psychiatric treatments and medication adherence.

Order baseline and rule-out workup before/alongside antipsychotic initiation: CBC, CMP (electrolytes, renal, hepatic), fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipid panel, TSH; pregnancy test when relevant; urine toxicology; consider prolactin baseline if using risperidone; consider ECG when QT risk factors/meds or syncope/palpitations.
Review results to assess medical contributors, metabolic baseline, and contraindications/precautions for antipsychotics; identify substance-induced psychosis risk.
Required critical safety check before prescribing any medication.
Required critical safety check before prescribing; evaluate interactions with current meds/substances (e.g., QT-prolonging agents, CYP inhibitors/inducers, sedatives).

Clarify relevant contraindication risk factors prior to ordering additional tests or initiating treatment: cardiac history/QT risk, seizure history, diabetes/obesity, EPS history, hyperprolactinemia symptoms, pregnancy/sexual activity when relevant.

Formulate working diagnosis (early-onset schizophrenia spectrum/first-episode psychosis) with differential (substance/medication-induced, mood disorder with psychotic features, PTSD, autism spectrum, ADHD, neurologic/medical causes) based on symptoms, impairment, and time course.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, uncertainty/differential and need to rule out causes; emphasize collateral/longitudinal assessment; address attention concerns and why psychosis is prioritized; shared decision-making with patient/caregivers; discuss stigma and functional goals.

Explain treatment options (antipsychotic rationale, expected timeline, titration, adherence, not stopping abruptly), side effects and urgent warning signs; include lifestyle and monitoring plan (metabolic, EPS, prolactin-related effects where relevant), and substance avoidance.

Initiate evidence-based first-line antipsychotic at a low dose with titration plan (e.g., aripiprazole or risperidone depending on patient factors), after completing critical safety checks; include PRN guidance only if appropriate and safe.

Arrange close follow-up (within 1 week for first-episode/med start), monitoring plan (weight/BMI, vitals, glucose/A1c, lipids, EPS/akathisia, prolactin symptoms), and safety plan/crisis resources; coordinate psychotherapy/family interventions and school supports.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and safety net instructions.

patient.chief_complaint: My kid keeps throwing up in the mornings and seems really off-balance.

volunteer: Throwing up most mornings for the past couple of weeks, sometimes without feeling sick first
Walking is wobbly and they’ve been stumbling more than usual
Headaches that seem worse when they first wake up
They’re more tired and not acting like themselves
Their legs seem weak and they don’t want to climb stairs (muscle weakness)
if_asked: They’ve been more cranky and hard to soothe, with sudden crying spells
They’ve had a couple of times where their eyes looked like they were ‘jerking’ or couldn’t focus for a few seconds
They’ve been eating less and have lost a little weight
They’ve been sleeping more during the day but still wake up tired
They’ve had a few falls that seemed out of proportion to what they were doing
They’ve been complaining that bright lights bother them
hidden: Their head seems like it’s grown faster lately compared to hats not fitting well (parent didn’t connect it to symptoms)
They sometimes tilt their head to one side when looking at things, like they’re trying to see better
They’ve had brief moments of staring and not responding for a few seconds, then acting normal
They seem to choke or cough more when drinking thin liquids, but the family thought it was just ‘drinking too fast’
They’ve been holding onto walls or furniture when walking, but the family thought it was just being cautious
resistant: They had an episode of wetting the bed after being dry for a long time, and the parent feels embarrassed and didn’t want to bring it up
The parent admits they delayed coming in because they were worried others would think they were overreacting
misleading: The parent thinks it might be a stomach bug or food intolerance because of the vomiting
They recently had a cold, so the parent wonders if it’s an ear infection causing the balance issues
The parent suspects migraines because headaches run in the family
They were given motion-sickness medicine once, and it seemed to help a little, so the parent thinks it’s ‘just dizziness’
noise: A mild runny nose on and off for the past week
A small bruise on the shin from a playground bump
Occasional belly aches after drinking milk

progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about strength, climbing stairs, and changes in activity.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about vision changes, eye movements, or episodes of looking ‘off.’

progressive_reveal.symptom: They’ve had a few seconds of staring and not responding, then they’re fine.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about spells, zoning out, or possible seizures.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Hats don’t fit like they used to; their head seems bigger than a few months ago.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about head growth, hat size changes, or pediatric growth concerns.

progressive_reveal.symptom: They’ve been coughing/choking more when drinking thin liquids.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks about swallowing, choking, or voice changes.

progressive_reveal.symptom: They wet the bed once after being dry for a long time.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Clinician asks gently about bladder changes and reassures it’s common to discuss.

general_concerns: Believes the child’s muscle weakness is due to 'not eating enough protein' or 'being lazy/tired,' rather than a neurologic problem.
Thinks brain tumors in children are usually caused by something the parent did (e.g., a fall, screen time, vaccines), and expresses guilt about this.
Assumes that if symptoms come and go, it cannot be something serious like a tumor.
Believes surgery will 'spread the cancer' or that biopsy is always dangerous and should be avoided.
Thinks chemotherapy is uniformly fatal or that it will permanently 'ruin the brain,' and asks if vitamins/herbal supplements could replace it.
Assumes radiation is never used in young children and therefore treatment options are limited to 'waiting' or 'palliative care.'
economic_concerns: Worries that imaging, hospitalization, and treatment will be unaffordable and that lack of insurance or high deductibles means care will be delayed.
Believes they may be denied treatment if they cannot pay upfront, and is hesitant to agree to recommended tests.
Fears job loss or reduced hours for the caregiver due to frequent appointments and hospital stays.
Assumes travel and parking costs will be overwhelming and asks if all care can be done locally even if specialists are recommended.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s anxious mother. Speak vaguely at first and focus on: “He keeps throwing up in the mornings and seems off-balance.” Volunteer that this has been happening most mornings for ~2 weeks, sometimes without nausea first; he’s wobbly/stumbling, has headaches worse on waking, is more tired/not himself, and his legs seem weak (avoids stairs). Mention you think it’s a stomach bug/food intolerance, or maybe an ear infection after a recent cold; migraines run in the family; motion-sickness medicine helped a little so you think it’s “just dizziness.”

Only if asked, add: cranky with sudden crying, brief eye “jerking”/can’t focus, eating less/weight loss, daytime sleepiness, falls, and bright light bothers him.

Do not volunteer: hats not fitting, head tilt, brief staring spells, coughing/choking on thin liquids, or holding walls—unless specifically questioned.

Resist mentioning one bedwetting episode unless asked directly; act embarrassed. Admit you delayed coming because you feared overreacting.

If the clinician is empathetic, soften, share worries about cost/insurance, fear of being denied care, losing work hours, and ask if testing/specialists can be local.

medications: Dexamethasone 1 mg PO/IV every 6 hours
Ondansetron 2 mg PO/IV every 8 hours as needed for nausea
Acetaminophen 160 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain/fever
confounders: Recent viral upper respiratory infection with decreased oral intake (can contribute to fatigue/weakness and mild hyponatremia)
Possible SIADH related to intracranial tumor causing hyponatremia and weakness
Corticosteroid exposure (dexamethasone) can transiently improve neurologic symptoms and mask progression

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT) of the central nervous system
acceptable_alternatives: Embryonal tumor of the CNS (pending molecular confirmation)
CNS malignant rhabdoid tumor (historical term)
required_evidence: Neuroimaging demonstrating an intracranial mass consistent with an aggressive pediatric CNS tumor (MRI brain/spine with contrast preferred)
Histopathology consistent with rhabdoid tumor morphology
Loss of INI1 (SMARCB1) nuclear expression on immunohistochemistry (or less commonly BRG1/SMARCA4 loss)
Clinical correlation with neurologic deficits (e.g., muscle weakness) attributable to tumor location or spinal involvement
Staging evaluation for leptomeningeal dissemination (MRI spine and CSF cytology when safe)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal at presentation; may be low if chronic disease or prior therapy/bleeding (no specific diagnostic pattern for AT/RT).
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: Often normal at presentation; may parallel hemoglobin if anemia present.
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal at presentation; may be elevated with stress/infection; not diagnostic.
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal at presentation; thrombocytopenia would raise concern for marrow involvement/other causes or treatment effects.
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low if SIADH or intracranial pathology-related dysnatremia; otherwise normal.
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Typically normal unless poor intake, vomiting, renal issues, or medication effects.
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Typically normal; may track with sodium and acid-base status.
expected_lab_results.CMP - Bicarbonate (CO2): Typically normal; may be low with dehydration/metabolic acidosis from vomiting/poor intake.

medications: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg PO/IV every 6 hours (max 4 mg per dose) for symptomatic cerebral edema; taper as clinically feasible
Levetiracetam 10 mg/kg PO/IV every 12 hours for seizure prophylaxis/treatment if seizures present or high risk; adjust per neurology
Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO/IV every 4-6 hours as needed for pain/fever (do not exceed weight-based daily maximum)
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to pediatric neurosurgery and pediatric neuro-oncology
Maximal safe surgical resection or debulking when feasible; obtain tissue for definitive diagnosis and molecular testing (SMARCB1/SMARCA4)
Staging: MRI brain and spine with contrast; CSF cytology when safe (avoid lumbar puncture if elevated intracranial pressure suspected)

Initiate multimodal oncologic therapy per pediatric neuro-oncology protocol: intensive multi-agent chemotherapy; consider high-dose chemotherapy with stem cell rescue in selected cases; radiotherapy planning individualized by age/tumor location (often deferred/modified in very young children)
Rehabilitation: physical/occupational therapy for muscle weakness and functional deficits; fall precautions
Supportive care planning: nutrition support, psychosocial support, palliative care involvement for symptom management and goals-of-care discussions
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate inpatient/urgent specialty evaluation (same day). Post-op oncology follow-up within 1 week; ongoing therapy visits weekly to biweekly during active treatment with MRI surveillance typically every 2-3 months initially per protocol.

must_include: AT/RT is a rare, aggressive brain/spinal cord tumor and requires urgent specialist care
Diagnosis is confirmed by tumor tissue testing, especially loss of INI1 (SMARCB1) or related markers
Muscle weakness can be caused by tumor effects on brain/spinal cord pathways and may change with swelling or progression
Treatment is multimodal: surgery when possible, plus intensive chemotherapy; radiation decisions depend on age and tumor spread
Short-term medicines (e.g., steroids) may be used to reduce brain swelling and improve symptoms; discuss taper and side effects
Need for staging tests (MRI spine, possible CSF testing) and why lumbar puncture may be delayed if pressure is high
Discuss prognosis honestly: aggressive tumor with significant risk, but outcomes depend on extent of resection, spread, and response to therapy
Review urgent warning signs requiring immediate care (worsening weakness, severe headache, vomiting, seizures, decreased alertness)
Clarify allergy history (amoxicillin rash) and ensure it is documented to avoid inappropriate antibiotic selection
Safety planning for weakness: fall precautions, supervised ambulation as needed, school/activity modifications
Importance of keeping follow-up appointments and adhering to therapy/rehab schedules
Infection prevention during chemotherapy planning (hand hygiene, fever plan, when to call/seek emergency care)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history: neurologic symptoms (weakness pattern, gait/falls, seizures), signs of raised intracranial pressure (headache, vomiting, lethargy), timeline/progression, prior imaging/biopsy status, current meds (especially steroids/antiemetics), and red-flag deterioration.
Clarify allergy history (amoxicillin rash details, timing, severity, anaphylaxis features) and document for future prescribing safety.

Order appropriate staging/assessment tests within scope: MRI brain review if not available, MRI spine for drop metastases, baseline labs for chemo planning (CBC, CMP), and consider CSF studies only if safe (avoid LP if elevated ICP suspected).

Confirm diagnostic evidence and staging results if available; identify findings suggesting elevated intracranial pressure or spinal involvement that guide urgency and contraindications to lumbar puncture.
State AT/RT as the working/confirmed diagnosis and explicitly note confirmation requires tumor tissue testing (INI1/SMARCB1 loss or related markers).
Explain diagnosis in family-friendly terms, including rarity/aggressiveness, urgent specialist care, tissue confirmation (INI1/SMARCB1), and why weakness can fluctuate with swelling/progression.

Explain treatment plan at a high level: multimodal therapy (surgery when feasible, intensive chemotherapy, age/spread-dependent radiation), role of steroids for edema with taper/side effects, and need for staging (MRI spine, possible CSF) with rationale for delaying LP if pressure high.

Address concerns: honest prognosis framing (depends on resection/spread/response), urgent warning signs requiring immediate care, and reiterate/document amoxicillin rash allergy to prevent inappropriate antibiotic selection.

Lifestyle/safety education: fall precautions and supervised ambulation, school/activity modifications, adherence to oncology/rehab follow-ups, and infection prevention/fever plan during chemotherapy planning.
Arrange urgent neuro-oncology/neurosurgery follow-up and appropriate imaging/rehab coordination; provide clear return precautions.
